coredata.dc:title: Hydrogen storage properties of V30–Ti–Cr–Fe alloys
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2006 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Yigang
Chen, Yungui
Liang, Hao
Wu, Chaoling
Tao, Mingda
coredata.dc:description: The hydrogen storage properties of V–Ti–Cr–Fe alloys with the compositions of V(30at.%)–Ti(15–55at.%)–Cr(7–43at.%)–Fe(2–18at.%) were studied in this paper. It was found that the hydrogen absorption capacities and the efficient hydrogen capacities of the alloys are dependent on the Ti/(Cr+Fe) ratio. The largest efficient hydrogen capacity is obtained at a Ti/(Cr+Fe) ratio of 1.0. When Ti/(Cr+Fe) ratio increases from 0.9 to 1.05, the lattice parameters of the alloys and the enthalpy of the hydrides grow, while the hydrogen desorption plateau pressure declines. The alloy of V30Ti35Cr25Fe10, which contains a high Fe content and a low V/Fe ratio of 3:1, exhibits a hydrogen capacity of 3.6wt.% and an efficient hydrogen capacity of 2.0wt.% at 298K.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogen storage materials
V-based solid solution
BCC alloy
Hydrogen storage properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Hydrogen storage properties of V30–Ti–Cr–Fe alloys

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: PCT curves of V30–Ti–Cr–Fe alloys at 313K during the desorption process. Numbers in bracket represent the ratio of Ti/(Cr+Fe).
Relationship between hydrogen storage capacities, efficient hydrogen capacity and Ti/(Cr+Fe) ratio.
Relationship between lattice parameters and Ti/(Cr+Fe) ratio.
PCT curves of V30–(Ti–Cr–Fe) alloys. ♦, ■, ▴, ● and *represent Ti/(Cr+Fe)=0.9, 0.95, 1.0, 1.05, 1.1, respectively.
X-ray diffraction patterns of the V30–Ti–Cr–Fe alloy.
Relation between Ti/(Cr+Fe) ratio and lattice parameters.
Relationship between desorption temperature and pressures.
Relationship between Ti/(Cr+Fe) ratio and enthalpy of hydride.
Compositions of the alloys by the uniform experiment method
Reported typical BCC alloys containing iron

article.ce:title: Hydrogen storage properties of V30–Ti–Cr–Fe alloys

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The hydrogen storage properties of V–Ti–Cr–Fe alloys with the compositions of V(30at.%)–Ti(15–55at.%)–Cr(7–43at.%)–Fe(2–18at.%) were studied in this paper. It was found that the hydrogen absorption capacities and the efficient hydrogen capacities of the alloys are dependent on the Ti/(Cr+Fe) ratio. The largest efficient hydrogen capacity is obtained at a Ti/(Cr+Fe) ratio of 1.0. When Ti/(Cr+Fe) ratio increases from 0.9 to 1.05, the lattice parameters of the alloys and the enthalpy of the hydrides grow, while the hydrogen desorption plateau pressure declines. The alloy of V30Ti35Cr25Fe10, which contains a high Fe content and a low V/Fe ratio of 3:1, exhibits a hydrogen capacity of 3.6wt.% and an efficient hydrogen capacity of 2.0wt.% at 298K.

ce:section.ce:para: The traditional hydrogen storage alloys, such as AB5-type, AB-type and AB2-type alloys, have the hydrogen capacities less than 2.0wt.% [1–3]. The magnesium-based alloys, such as Mg and Mg2Ni [4,5], have the higher capacities theoretically, but only absorb and desorb hydrogen at a high temperature of 300–400°C, which show good kinetics property and higher desorption hydrogen capacity at lower temperatures after being mechanically alloyed into nanostructure powders, catalyzed with transitional metals and metal oxides, or combined on nanometer scale with other alloys such as LaNi5 [6–10]. However, magnesium-based alloys are also very far from application. Vanadium-based solid solution alloys with BCC structures show the largest hydrogen capacity of 3.8wt.% [11], and have been brought to popular concerns.

It was reported [12–14] that the alloy of Ti25V35Cr40 could desorb about 2.6wt.% after heat-treatment, and Ti28Cr52V20 alloy shows an efficient capacity of 2.6wt.%. However, these alloys were all prepared by pure vanadium. Due to the high price of pure vanadium, even a very low content of vanadium of 2.5–10mol% or using other metals, such as Ta, Mo, and Nb, [15] to substitute V partly, the total cost of the production of such alloys is also very high. Commercial ferrovanadium alloy is a kind of cheaper vanadium sources comparatively. So developing the vanadium-based alloys containing iron is the base of obtaining cheap vanadium-based solid solution alloys with high capacity. Thus, the alloys of V–Ti–Fe have ever been studied. V49Ti43.5Fe7.5 alloy [16] could absorb 3.9wt.% of hydrogen at 253K and desorb 2.4wt.% at 573K, but the temperature for absorption is too low and too high for desorption. Singh [17] reported that V0.85Ti0.10Fe0.05 alloy contains a high vanadium content, which absorbs 1.6 H/M of hydrogen and desorbs 1.0 H/M at 30°C. V–Ti–Fe alloys have a high desorption capacity, but a low desorption capacity at moderate temperatures. Recently, Taizhong Huang and his co-workers [18] reported that TiCr1.2 (V-Fe) 0.6 alloy could absorb 3.2wt.% H and desorb 2.0wt.% H at 273K. Xuebing Yu and his co-workers [19] reported that a Ti–20Cr–12Mn–24V–5Fe alloy could absorb 3.32wt.% H and desorb 2.2wt.% H at 333K. The facts indicate that the V-based BCC alloys containing Fe might show good hydrogen absorption–desorption properties under moderate conditions.
In this paper, a series of V–Ti–Cr–Fe alloys was systematically investigated.

The purities of the raw materials, including vanadium, titanium, chromium and iron in this experiment were 99.9, 99.6, 99.9, and 99.8wt.%, respectively. The samples were arc-melted into button shaped ingots under an argon atmosphere. Samples were remelted for four times to ensure the homogeneity. No heat treatment was performed. Samples were crushed into 100-mesh powders in air, which were used for pressure–composition–temperature (PCT) tests.

PCT curves were measured using a Sieverts-type apparatus. Initial activation treatments were as follows. Each sample was put into a stainless steel reactor and was evacuated at 313k for 30min, and then at 673K for 30min. Later, hydrogen with a pressure of 4MPa was introduced into the reactor, which was kept for 30min and then cooled down to room temperature slowly. Finally, the samples were evacuated for 2h at 673K to ensure that the alloys were dehydrogenized completely for PCT measurement.
In this paper, the efficient hydrogen storage capacity is defined as the amount of hydrogen desorbed when the hydrogen pressure decreased from 4 to 0.01MPa.
The crystal structures and lattice parameters of these alloys were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis using Cu Kα radiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrogen storage properties of V30–Ti–Cr–Fe alloys
ce:section.ce:para: The content of V was fixed at 30at.%, and the compositions of different V30–Ti–Cr–Fe alloys were designed by the uniform experimental design method (as shown in Table 1 ).

Fig. 1 shows the PCT curves of the alloys (Fe1–Fe9) at 313K during the desorption process. The PCT curves of all alloys seem to be inclined lines and no plateau regions exist except for alloy Fe5, which shows an obvious plateau region. The hydrogen capacities of the alloys vary greatly with the compositions, as is shown in Fig. 1. The influence of the compositions on the hydrogen capacities can be contributed to Ti/(Cr+Fe) ratio. The relationships between the hydrogen storage capacities, or efficient hydrogen capacities, and Ti/(Cr+Fe) ratio are shown in Fig. 2 . The hydrogen storage capacities increase almost linearly with the ratio of Ti/(Cr+Fe) when Ti/(Cr+Fe) <1.0, and become nearly constant when Ti/(Cr+Fe) ≥1.0. The efficient hydrogen storage capacities increase with the increase of Ti/(Cr+Fe) at first, and then decrease rapidly, which reach the maximum at Ti/(Cr+Fe)=1.0.

Fig. 3 shows the XRD patterns of V30–Ti–Cr–Fe alloy powders. Single-phase structures were found in the alloys except for those when Ti/(Cr+Fe)=0.75 or 0.27, in which minor laves phases were observed. When the lattice parameters are concerned, it was found that they increase almost linearly with the ratio of Ti/(Cr+Fe), as is shown in Fig. 4 . It is well known that the plateau pressures drop with the increase of the lattice parameters. The alloys Fe2, Fe3, Fe6 and Fe9 have larger lattice parameters, which lead to very low plateau pressures (as the right four PCT lines seen in Fig. 1). Owing to the low plateau pressures, these alloys exhibit high hydrogen storage capacities but low efficient ones.

While the alloys Fe1, Fe4, Fe7 and Fe8 have smaller lattice parameters, thus they might have higher plateau pressures above 4MPa (as the left four PCT lines seen in Fig. 1). Owing to the high plateau pressures, these alloys also show low hydrogen storage capacities, and then low efficient ones. The lattice parameter, about 0.304nm, of the V30Ti35Cr25Fe10 alloy (Fe5) might be a proper constant to good hydrogen absorption–desorption performances of V-based BCC alloys.

Ti/(Cr+Fe) ratio is an important factor which influences the hydrogen storage properties of V–Ti–Cr–Fe alloys, as was analyzed above. In this section, Cr/Fe ratio was fixed at 2.5, and the effect of Ti/(Cr+Fe) ratio on the PCT properties of V–Ti–Cr–Fe alloys was investigated further.

The PCT curves of the alloys with different Ti/(Cr+Fe) ratio during the desorption process (at 298K) are shown in Fig. 5 . The plateau pressures of the alloys rise rapidly as the ratio deceases. When Ti/(Cr+Fe)=1.1, the plateau pressure is so low that no complete plateau region can be observed, and it is higher than 0.1MPa when Ti/(Cr+Fe)=0.9. The hydrogen storage capacities of the alloys are influenced little when the ratio grows from 0.9 to 1.1. While the efficient hydrogen storage capacities vary, which increase slowly with the ratio up to 1.0 and reduce rapidly when it increases further. The efficient hydrogen capacities reach a maximum of 2.0wt.% when Ti/(Cr+Fe)=1.0.

To understand the effect of Ti/(Cr+Fe) ratio on the hydride stability, the Van’t Hoff curves of the alloys with the ratio of 0.9–1.05 were obtained by testing PCT curves at 298, 313 and 333K, respectively (as seen in Fig. 8 ).
It can be seen in Fig. 9 that the hydride formation enthalpy increases linearly with the increase of Ti/(Cr+Fe) ratio, which indicates that the hydrides become more stable when the ratio increases.

Some researchers [20–22] reported that there is a dependence of the maximum hydrogen storage capacity of multi-component BCC alloys on the ratio of electron-to-atom (e/a), and exists a critical value e/a=5.1. The maximum hydrogen storage capacity declines when the value of e/a exceeds 5.1. The great decrease of the maximum hydrogen storage capacity of Ti0.16Zr0.05Cr0.22V0.57 alloy after the addition of Fe was partly attributed to the increase of e/a caused by the addition of Fe. The value of e/a reaches 5.16 for the addition of 5at.% Fe, and the corresponding hydrogen storage capacity is only about 2.2wt.%, which is much less than that of Ti0.16Zr0.05Cr0.22V0.57 (3.55wt.%). However, this behavior hasn’t been found in this paper. The values of e/a exceed 5.1 in the alloys of V30–Ti–Cr–Fe (Cr/Fe=2.5) with Ti/(Cr+Fe) ratio of 0.9–1.1. These alloys all have hydrogen storage capacities higher than 3.4wt.%. In our opinions, the lattice parameter is an important factor to influence the hydrogen storage capacities and the efficient ones. Large lattice parameter contributes to high hydrogen storage capacity but low plateau pressure, but small lattice parameter leads to high plateau pressure but low hydrogen storage capacity. Only moderate lattice parameter helps to obtain high hydrogen storage capacity, moderate plateau pressure and high efficient hydrogen storage capacity.

In this paper, a V30Ti35Cr25Fe10 alloy with performances better than other reported ones (as seen in Table 2 ) was developed, which can absorb 3.6wt.% H and desorb 2.0wt.% H at 298K. The efficient hydrogen storage capacity in Table 2 is defined as the desorption capacity above 0.01MPa. It should be noted that this alloy has a high Fe content of 10at.% and a low ratio of V/Fe about 3:1. The ratio of V/Fe is about 5:1 in commercial 80VFe master alloy. Thus, it is promising to use ferrovanadium alloys as the vanadium source.

The hydrogen storage properties of the V30–Ti–Cr–Fe quaternary alloys were studied. The conclusions were as follows:
(1)
The hydrogen absorption capacities and the effective hydrogen capacities of the alloys are largely dependent on the ratio of Ti/(Cr+Fe).
(2)
The alloy showed the largest effective hydrogen capacity at a Ti/(Cr+Fe) ratio of 1.0. With the increase of Ti/(Cr+Fe) ratio (0.9–1.05), the lattice parameters of the alloys and the enthalpy of the hydrides grow, and the hydrogen desorbing plateau pressure declines.
(3)
The alloy of V30Ti35Cr25Fe10 with 10at.% Fe exhibits a hydrogen capacity of 3.6wt.% and an effective hydrogen capacity of 2.0wt.% at 298K.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Key Project of Science and Technology of Panzhihua City (GX2003-1), Sichuan Province, China.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Ming Au, F. Pourarian, S.G. Sankar, W.E. Wallace, Lian Zhang, Mater. Sci. Eng. B33 (1995) 53–57.
Taizhong Huang, Zhu Wu, Baojia Xia, Jinzhou Chen, Xuebin Yu, Naixin Xu, Changwei Lu, Huimei Yu, Sci. Technol. Adv. Mater. 4 (2003) 491–494.
